title: btc-graph content source audit
objective: Audit Bitcoin educational sources and expand btc-graph with atomic concepts and strict prerequisite edges.
notes: Canonical source of truth for the content discovery loop. Update this JSON first, then regenerate the workbook.

sources.name: Bitcoin Optech Topics
sources.scope: Topic index covering protocol, wallet, Lightning, fee, relay, and operational concepts.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the index views plus individual topic pages for canonical naming, aliases, see-also links, and primary docs.
sources.notes: 157 topics plus 117 aliases make this a naming registry and gap detector more than a bulk node source. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.x-only-public-keys, protocol.version-3-transaction-relay, protocol.tapscript, dev.taproot-psbt-fields, protocol.accidental-confiscation, dev.taproot-descriptors, dev.descriptor-wallet-policies, dev.descriptors-in-psbt, mining.block-withholding, and protocol.replacement-cycling.

sources.name: BTC Transcripts Topics
sources.scope: Topic index into transcript-backed technical discussions and workshops.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track Topics, Categories, Types, and Sources as separate provenance surfaces before drilling into individual transcript families.
sources.notes: Use mainly for provenance and terminology normalization. Topic labels should not be imported 1:1.

sources.name: Bitcoin Dev Philosophy
sources.scope: Conceptual and systems-thinking source likely to surface bridge concepts and framing gaps.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track 9 chapter-level rows first, with later splits for dense chapters 5, 7, 8, and 9.
sources.notes: Complete nine-chapter pass. Dense chapters 5, 7, 8, and 9 were split conceptually and deduplicated against the graph. Implemented bridge concepts: fundamentals.validation-sovereignty, protocol.consensus-vs-policy, protocol.chain-split-risk, dev.review-culture, dev.permissionless-development, dev.pseudonymous-development, dev.bootstrappable-builds, mining.miner-decentralization, and dev.selection-cryptography. Funding, culture shock, scaling taxonomies, and historical incident examples remain contextual or existing coverage rather than standalone gaps.

sources.name: Mastering Bitcoin Book Map
sources.scope: Structured chapter map for foundational and implementation-oriented concept coverage.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track 14 chapter rows first, grouping chapters 4 to 12 as the primary extraction surface.
sources.notes: Complete 14-chapter outline pass. Chapter titles were treated as containers, then prerequisite ladders and bridge concepts were normalized against the existing graph. Implemented missing bridges: protocol.light-client-trust-model, ops.full-node-vs-lightweight-wallet, custody.coin-selection, protocol.change-outputs, protocol.bech32m-addresses, protocol.script-execution-stack, dev.extended-keys, protocol.witness-structure, protocol.partial-merkle-proofs, protocol.pay-to-contract, extension.single-use-seals, and extension.client-side-validation.

sources.name: Optech Schnorr/Taproot Workshop
sources.scope: Focused advanced curriculum around Schnorr, Taproot, and related prerequisite chains.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the 12 module pages plus shared assets once per source.
sources.notes: Complete 12-module pass, including preparation/math, Schnorr/MuSig, Taproot, Tapscript, TapTree, Huffman construction, and the degrading-multisig case study. Implemented bridge concepts: protocol.tagged-hashes, protocol.taptweak, protocol.taproot-key-path-spends, protocol.taptree, protocol.tapleaf-inclusion-proofs, protocol.taproot-control-blocks, protocol.huffman-optimized-taptrees, and custody.degrading-multisig.

sources.name: Decoding by Bitcoin Devs
sources.scope: Structured educational material that may reveal curriculum-style prerequisite bundles.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the Introduction, Tools, Transactions, Scripts, and Taproot module groups before lesson-level expansion.
sources.notes: Best used for sequencing and beginner-friendly prerequisite ordering rather than broad topic discovery. Implemented from this source in the current branch: protocol.p2pk, security.nonce-reuse-attack, protocol.p2pkh, protocol.fee-calculation, protocol.op-checkmultisig, protocol.p2ms, protocol.bare-multisig-standardness, and protocol.musig. Current tracked module queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Programming Lightning
sources.scope: Interactive Lightning curriculum covering payment channels, channel state updates, HTLCs, onion routing, routing economics, and visual Lightning simulations.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the Lightning Tutorial, Onion Routing Tutorial, and Visual Lightning surfaces as separate course clusters before drilling into individual lessons.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. The public app bundle clearly exposes Lightning Tutorial, Onion Routing Tutorial, and Visual Lightning as the main extraction surfaces, which should produce bridge concepts in the payment-channel and routing branches without depending on transcript-style provenance. Implemented from this source in the current branch: lightning.per-commitment-secrets, lightning.obscured-commitment-number, lightning.commitment-output-scripts, and lightning.htlc-output-scripts. Current tracked surface queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Lightning Network BOLTs
sources.scope: Canonical Lightning wire protocol, transport, channel, transaction, routing, discovery, feature-negotiation, invoice, and offer specifications.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track every current BOLT document as an enumeration cluster. Extract only atomic mechanisms and deduplicate against existing Lightning nodes before proposing additions.
sources.notes: Complete first-pass enumeration of all 12 current BOLT documents (00, 01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 07, 08, 09, 10, 11, and 12). Existing commitment, closure, gossip, fee-policy, invoice, and offer nodes were confirmed; lightning.short-channel-id was added as the only missing learner-facing bridge. Message names, TLV fields, and query workflows remain inside their parent protocol concepts rather than becoming implementation-shaped nodes.

sources.name: Bitcoin Improvement Proposals
sources.scope: Canonical Bitcoin Improvement Proposals covering process, consensus rules, signatures, wallet formats, applications, peer services, relay, APIs, mining, and deployment mechanisms.
sources.clusterStrategy: Enumerate the repository registry first, then group BIPs into consensus soft forks, signature encoding and serialization, script and output templates, wallet and payment standards, and peer/relay protocols. Treat BIPs as specification provenance rather than automatic one-to-one nodes.
sources.notes: Complete first-pass registry enumeration across process, consensus/deployment, signatures/serialization, script/output templates, wallet/payment standards, peer/relay protocols, and emerging application proposals. Implemented from this source: protocol.strict-der-signatures, custody.deterministic-multisig-key-sorting, protocol.generic-signed-messages, protocol.proof-of-payment, and protocol.pay-to-anchor. BIP 329 Wallet Labels merges into custody.output-labeling; BIP 431 TRUC confirms protocol.version-3-transaction-relay. Draft and closed proposals are retained as provenance, not treated as deployed consensus rules.

sources.name: Mempool.space Documentation
sources.scope: Operational documentation for fee visibility, mempool behavior, transaction monitoring, APIs, and self-hosting. Treat it as implementation and observability guidance, not consensus authority.
sources.clusterStrategy: Enumerate the FAQ, REST API, WebSocket API, and self-hosting/repository clusters first. Use Bitcoin Core documentation as the canonical authority for consensus and policy semantics, then retain Mempool.space-specific observability concepts only when they survive deduplication.
sources.notes: Complete FAQ/mempool, REST API, WebSocket/live-events, and self-hosting pass. ops.mempool-purging is the only learner-facing concept added from this source; explorer UI, REST/WebSocket endpoints, and self-hosting are recorded as implementation/product surfaces rather than Bitcoin graph nodes. Bitcoin Core policy and RPC documentation remain canonical for protocol semantics.

sources.name: Cypherpunk Library Bitcoin-Adjacent Canon
sources.scope: Bitcoin-adjacent cypherpunk readings focused on privacy, electronic cash, monetary theory, and the ideological roots most likely to sharpen foundational and history nodes.
sources.clusterStrategy: Start with four clusters: cypherpunk manifestos, privacy and surveillance essays, eCash and digital-cash texts, and monetary-theory readings. Defer clearly non-Bitcoin-adjacent political or general-hacker texts unless they surface a direct graph gap.
sources.notes: Added from user direction. High-signal starting texts from the collection include A Cypherpunk's Manifesto, The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto, Your Secret Right to Cash, Protecting Privacy with Electronic Cash, The Beauty of eCash, The Cyphernomicon, Ideal Money and Asymptotically Ideal Money, and 21 Lessons. Implemented from this source in the current branch: history.chaumian-ecash, privacy.secret-right-to-cash, economics.ideal-money. Merged into existing coverage in the current branch: history.cypherpunks via A Cypherpunk's Manifesto; history.bitcoin-anarchism via The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto. Current tracked cluster queue is exhausted for this pass.

sources.name: Bitcoin Dev Project Explained Posts
sources.scope: Short-form educational posts from @Bitcoin_Devs that explicitly explain protocol, wallet, and Lightning mechanisms in learner-facing language.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the live X search as one source-family row first, then expand to one row per matching post once current results are enumerated. Group matching posts by concept family such as hashing, fee management, node architecture, channel mechanics, and upgrade proposals.
sources.notes: Completed for the current authenticated enumeration on July 11, 2026: 34 matching posts are tracked individually below and each has a normalized candidate classification. The earlier July 8 pass reported 43 posts but did not preserve one source row per post; that historical discrepancy is documented in Decisions and is not treated as evidence for nine additional unobserved posts. Existing coverage includes time-warp attack, duplicate transaction bug, 64-byte transaction bug, nonce reuse, Lamport signatures, and Shamir's Secret Sharing.

sources.name: 101 Perguntas e Respostas sobre Bitcoin - 1a edicao

sources.notes: Added Spark as a distinct implementation/protocol node downstream of Statechains; operator coordination and Lightning interoperability remain inside the Spark concept rather than becoming separate nodes.

sources.scope: Cube's Bitcoin-native Layer 2, CubeVM, ZKTLCs, timeout-tree ownership virtualization, unilateral exits, Shadowing, Projector, lifting, and data-availability encoding.
sources.clusterStrategy: Track the article's named sections as one source cluster, separating reusable learner-facing primitives from Cube-specific implementation mechanisms and speculative research directions.
sources.notes: Added Cube, Timeout Trees, and ZKTLCs. concepts.title: Tagged Hashes
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 0.3 Tagged Hashes
concepts.atomicityRationale: A narrow cryptographic construction repeatedly used in Taproot and Schnorr material, distinct from generic hash functions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base hash-function mental model before namespace-separated tagged hashing makes sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: X-Only Public Keys
concepts.sourceContext: Topics index and Taproot-related topic pages
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a specific key representation concept that bridges Schnorr math to Taproot output keys.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The concept depends on both general key knowledge and the Schnorr/Taproot context in which x-only keys matter.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: TapTweak
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.2 Taptweak
concepts.atomicityRationale: A concrete Taproot mechanism that is smaller than Taproot as a whole and useful for gap diagnosis.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The tweak mechanism is a Taproot-specific key derivation step that directly depends on Taproot context, tagged hashing, and x-only key encoding.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: Taproot Key-Path Spends
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop modules 2.0 Introduction to Taproot and 2.1 Segwit V1
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific Taproot spending mode: spending by signature against the tweaked output key without revealing a script path. It is narrower than Taproot overall, narrower than TapTweak as a key-derivation mechanism, and distinct from Tapscript's script-path execution rules.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is understanding that the spend authorizes against the tweaked Taproot output key. Schnorr signatures and the broader Taproot context are already inherited through TapTweak, while script-path concepts such as Tapscript and TapTree are sibling alternatives rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest remaining workshop-native Taproot spending-mode slice after ruling out broader wrappers and keeping script-path detail in the Tapscript and TapTree branches.

concepts.title: TapTree
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.4 Taptree
concepts.atomicityRationale: Specific Taproot script-tree structure, distinct from generic Merkle trees and from Taproot overall.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: TapTree is best taught immediately after TapTweak. Merkle-tree knowledge remains useful context but is already reached transitively through the Taproot path, so the direct edge would be redundant.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: TapLeaf Inclusion Proofs
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.4 Taptree
concepts.atomicityRationale: A concrete proof object within script-path spends, narrower than Taproot and narrower than TapTree.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The proof only makes sense after the learner understands the TapTree structure it proves against.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as part of the first Taproot-internal bridge slice.

concepts.title: Huffman-Optimized TapTrees
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 2.5 Huffman Construction
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is a narrow optimization concept that explains one specific design tradeoff inside Taproot trees.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The optimization is about arranging TapTree leaves, so the base tree concept is direct.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the last explicit pending workshop optimization concept.

concepts.title: Degrading Multisig
concepts.sourceContext: Workshop module 3.1 Degrading Multisig
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific timeout-based custody policy, narrower than generic multisig, narrower than threshold signatures, and narrower than broad inheritance or recovery planning.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the baseline multisig model and timelock semantics first. Taproot is a clean implementation path surfaced by the workshop, but the custody policy itself can be understood before committing to a specific script-path mechanism. Generic backup planning is a downstream use context rather than a prerequisite for understanding this script-policy construction.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next workshop-derived custody case study, while leaving broader threshold-signature umbrella material for later and keeping Taproot as implementation context rather than a direct prerequisite.

concepts.title: Chain Split Risk
concepts.sourceContext: Upgrading chapter 5.3.1 Costs of a split
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific upgrade-risk concept about the costs of incompatible rule sets continuing in parallel, not a broad deployment-mechanisms umbrella.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the compatibility distinction between soft forks and hard forks before the consequences of a persistent split make sense.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow upgrade-risk bridge between protocol.softfork-hardfork and the existing activation-history case studies.

concepts.title: Tapscript
concepts.sourceContext: Topics alias normalization around Tapscript and Taproot script-path rules
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is the specific Taproot-era script ruleset, narrower than Taproot and cleaner than a broad script-path-spends explainer.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is the Taproot spend context. The base Script model is already inherited transitively through the SegWit and transaction-lifecycle path, so adding protocol.script directly would be redundant in the current graph.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the next additive Taproot bridge without rewiring older phase-locked descendants such as protocol.op-checksigadd.

concepts.title: Taproot PSBT Fields
concepts.sourceContext: PSBT topic page plus linked implementation notes around BIP371 support for Taproot inputs and outputs
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is the narrow PSBT extension surface for Taproot-specific fields, distinct from general PSBT workflows, descriptors, and MuSig-specific coordination formats.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the base PSBT exchange model and the Taproot spend model first. A direct dev.psbt-v2 edge would overfit the node because BIP371 extends both PSBTv0 and PSBTv2 rather than depending on v2 specifically.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow wallet-development bridge between generic PSBT workflows and Taproot-specific signing metadata.

concepts.title: Accidental Confiscation
concepts.sourceContext: Optech topic page on soft-fork designs that can accidentally make old funds unspendable
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific upgrade-risk failure mode about spendability loss under new soft-fork restrictions, not a broad activation or governance wrapper.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the soft-fork restriction model before this failure mode makes sense. The chain-split and standardness-policy branches add useful context but are not direct prerequisites for the core concept.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a narrow upgrade-risk leaf after protocol.chain-split-risk, without forcing policy or activation-history rewires.

concepts.title: Coin Selection
concepts.sourceContext: Chapter 2 overview: making change and coin selection during wallet transaction construction
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific wallet decision layer about choosing which UTXOs fund a payment, narrower than wallet management and broader than any one privacy heuristic or RPC surface.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The direct learner need is fee pressure and transaction-size tradeoffs. The UTXO model is already inherited transitively through the fee-market branch, and manual coin control is a downstream application of coin selection rather than a prerequisite.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as a wallet-construction bridge that parents several existing privacy and funding leaves conceptually, without rewiring those older phase-locked nodes yet.

concepts.title: Change Outputs
concepts.sourceContext: Chapter 2 wallet transaction construction flow: making change before coin selection and later privacy heuristics
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific transaction-construction concept: returning excess input value to the spender as a new output. It is narrower than generic outputs and narrower than the later wallet-policy and privacy decisions built on top of it.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the basic output model first. Transaction-lifecycle is broader context already inherited in the output branch, and coin selection is a downstream wallet decision that depends on understanding change outputs rather than preceding it.
concepts.notes: Implemented in the current branch as the missing bridge between addresses-outputs and the existing coin-selection and change-privacy leaves.

concepts.title: Taproot Descriptors

concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates the tr() descriptor layer for Taproot outputs, narrower than generic descriptors and narrower than PSBT or MuSig workflow integrations.

concepts.title: Human-Readable Bitcoin Addresses
concepts.sourceContext: Payment request formats cluster: Human readable addresses
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific payment UX concept: readable handles that resolve to normal payment instructions through a lookup system. This is narrower than general address formats, narrower than LNURL, and narrower than reusable-address protocols like silent payments.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: The clean direct parent is the privacy problem created by reusable payment identifiers. The underlying address model still matters, but it is already inherited transitively through privacy.address-reuse, while LNURL and silent payments remain neighboring implementations or downstream examples rather than prerequisites.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest first Bitcoin Design gap because it is highly user-relevant and bridges wallet UX with privacy/trust tradeoffs.

concepts.title: Transaction Metadata
concepts.sourceContext: Daily spending wallet cluster: Activity plus Contacts
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific wallet-layer concept: contextual information such as names, notes, tags, and linked contacts attached to payments or activity rows. This is narrower than generic transaction history and narrower than UTXO labeling, because it focuses on user-facing meaning and portability rather than spend-control metadata alone.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the existing concept of wallet-applied labels plus the privacy idea that transaction history creates pseudonymous patterns. Human-readable addresses are a common input to richer metadata, but they are a neighboring UX mechanism rather than a prerequisite for understanding that wallet metadata exists and must be portable.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest next design-side slice because it adds a clear wallet-data concept without collapsing into generic backup flows.

concepts.title: Multiple Wallets
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Design guide page: Multiple Wallets
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific wallet-architecture concept: separating funds into distinct wallets for different purposes, owners, or security models inside one application. This is narrower than generic wallet UX, narrower than HD account-path mechanics, and narrower than backup strategy discussions.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners first need the operational idea that custody decisions shift responsibility and risk management onto the user. Multisig and watch-only wallets are important examples inside multi-wallet setups, and HD wallets can implement the structure, but those are neighboring wallet types or mechanisms rather than prerequisites for understanding purpose-based wallet separation itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen over encrypted cloud backups and wallet-encryption because the design page is really about custody architecture and purposeful wallet separation, not about backup transport or at-rest key protection.

concepts.title: Progressive Security
concepts.sourceContext: Bitcoin Design reference design: Upgradeable wallet
concepts.atomicityRationale: One specific custody-architecture concept: deliberately staging stronger key-management schemes as holdings, confidence, and risk increase. This is narrower than generic wallet security, narrower than any single scheme such as cloud backup or multisig, and narrower than multiple-wallet UI organization.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Learners need the concrete upgrade targets first: external signing devices and multi-key wallet schemes. Self-custody is already inherited through both, while generic backup hygiene is a neighboring concern that the progressive-security path evaluates rather than a prerequisite for understanding the staged-upgrade pattern itself.

concepts.title: Transaction Rebroadcast Privacy

concepts.title: TXID and WTXID

concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific identifier distinction: txid commits to the base transaction serialization while wtxid commits to the witness-inclusive serialization. Malleability and relay behavior are important consequences and use cases, but they are not required to understand the identifier distinction itself.
concepts.notes: Chosen as the cleanest remaining Academy transaction-format bridge after checking that the distinction is not already owned by transaction malleability or WTXID relay.

concepts.title: Fee Calculation

concepts.title: Bech32m Addresses

concepts.title: Script Execution Stack

concepts.title: Your Secret Right to Cash

Neither parent makes the other redundant.

concepts.title: Ideal Money

concepts.title: Bitcoin URI Scheme (BIP21)
concepts.sourceContext: Payment Processing -> Requesting Payments -> bitcoin: URI

concepts.title: Hardened Keys

concepts.title: Obscured Commitment Number

concepts.title: HTLC Output Scripts

concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific BOLT 3 mechanism layer: the offered and received HTLC witness scripts embedded in commitment transactions. concepts.title: Duplicate Transaction Bug
concepts.sourceContext: Duplicate transaction bug explained: BIP 30 -> BIP 34 -> BIP 54
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one specific consensus bug: duplicated transactions with identical txids, especially duplicated coinbases, breaking the assumption that txids uniquely identify prior outputs. concepts.title: Lamport Signatures

concepts.title: Shamir's Secret Sharing
concepts.sourceContext: Shamir's secret sharing explained simply

It is narrower than generic backup and recovery, narrower than multisig signer coordination, and distinct from XOR-based shard schemes.

concepts.title: Transaction Witness Structure
concepts.sourceContext: Transactions > Witness Structure > Witness Structure Serialization
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific transaction-format concept: how each input carries a witness stack and how the marker, flag, item counts, and witness items are serialized. concepts.title: Partial Merkle Proofs
concepts.sourceContext: The Blockchain > Merkle Trees and Lightweight Clients
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific client-verification mechanism: sending only the relevant Merkle branches and traversal bits needed to prove a transaction belongs to a block. It is narrower than Merkle trees, block structure, and the broader light-client trust model, and distinct from compact block filters.
concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Block structure supplies the Merkle-root context and already inherits the generic Merkle-tree concept. concepts.notes: Implemented as the missing legacy-SPV proof mechanism between block structure and the existing light-client trust model. Compact block filters remain a distinct modern client-sync approach rather than a duplicate.

concepts.title: Review Culture in Bitcoin Development
concepts.sourceContext: Open Source chapter > Review
concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific development-process concept: how independent review across proposal, implementation, deployment, and adoption stages reduces correctness, security, and compatibility risk. It is narrower than the BIPs process and distinct from general adversarial thinking, reproducible builds, and responsible disclosure.

concepts.title: Lightning Feature Flags

concepts.title: Lightning Onion Messages

concepts.title: Strict DER Signature Encoding

concepts.title: Deterministic Multisig Key Sorting
concepts.sourceContext: BIP 67 > deterministic sorting of compressed public keys in multisig redeem scripts

concepts.title: Mempool Purging and Rolling Minimum Fee

concepts.atomicityRationale: This is one specific node-operations behavior: how mempool pressure raises a rolling minimum relay feerate and causes lower-feerate transactions to be evicted. It is narrower than mempool policy, fee markets, package relay, and generic transaction confirmation.

concepts.title: Generic Signed Message Format
concepts.sourceContext: BIP 322: Generic Signed Message Format
concepts.atomicityRationale: This isolates one interoperability format: proving that a signer can satisfy a Bitcoin scriptPubKey for a message through virtual transactions. concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Ordinary RBF supplies the direct replacement-policy model. Package relay describes propagation, cluster mempool describes internal package accounting, submitpackage describes local submission, and transaction pinning is a motivating risk rather than a technical requirement for understanding package replacement rules.
concepts.notes: Added after deduping against protocol.rbf, dev.submitpackage-rpc, protocol.package-relay, protocol.cluster-mempool, and transaction-pinning concepts. Current policy scope is limited 1P1C-style package replacement rather than unrestricted package RBF.

concepts.prerequisiteRationale: Pool shares provide the direct mechanism: a pool pays for easier-target proofs but only a block-valid share earns the network reward. Pool organization and mining economics are inherited through that node; a majority attack is a distinct threat model and not required.

concepts.prerequisiteRationale: RBF supplies the replacement mechanism and CPFP supplies the child-fee sequence. Transaction pinning is the broader risk class explained by this node, while anchor outputs are one affected Lightning construction.

edges.notes: Non-redundant alongside dev.descriptors because neither concept implies the other in the current graph.

edges.rationale: The node is about carrying descriptor information during transaction coordination, so learners need the descriptor syntax and script-template model first.
edges.notes: Direct parent kept at the representation layer rather than the wallet-policy or taproot-specific refinements.

edges.rationale: Descriptor-aware exchange only makes sense after the learner understands the general PSBT coordination workflow that transports signing-related data between tools.
edges.notes: Direct parent kept at the base PSBT workflow level instead of requiring later version-specific extensions.

edges.rationale: The policy only makes sense once the learner understands ordinary multisig threshold custody and why signers can be split into live and backup sets.
edges.notes: Direct custody parent for the signer-threshold side of the construction.

edges.rationale: The degrading policy activates its weaker backup path only after a timeout, so timelock semantics are a direct part of the construction.
edges.notes: Needed alongside custody.multisig because the learner must understand the temporal rule, not just the signer policy.

edges.rationale: Understanding whether an absolute fee is high or low requires relating it to the blockspace pricing unit, so learners need weight and virtual bytes before fee calculation is fully meaningful in practice.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node at the mechanism layer without drifting into broader fee-market competition.

edges.rationale: Bech32m is an address-encoding format, so learners need the general relationship between addresses, script templates, and spendability before the newer checksum format is meaningful.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node on encoding semantics rather than on raw string-format trivia.

edges.rationale: Bech32m became necessary for witness version 1 and higher, and Taproot is the first major deployed witness-v1 use case, so learners need that upgrade context before the format distinction makes sense.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node anchored on the practical protocol transition that exposed Bech32m to users.

edges.rationale: The stack execution model is the direct mechanism by which Bitcoin Script evaluates spending conditions, so learners need the umbrella Script concept before drilling into stack behavior.
edges.notes: Direct parent positions the node as the conceptual middle layer between Script and opcode-specific leaves.

edges.rationale: Per-commitment secrets are generated for each new commitment state, so learners need the commitment-transaction model before the secret-rotation mechanism makes sense.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node on state evolution rather than on later punishment or watchtower behavior.

edges.rationale: The to_local and to_remote scripts rely on per-state key derivation, revocation keys, and delayed-payment keys, so learners need the per-commitment secret machinery first.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node at the commitment-key layer instead of collapsing back into the broader commitment-transaction overview.

edges.rationale: The delayed to_local branch is defined by a CSV-enforced waiting period, so learners need the relative-timelock opcode before the script template makes sense.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the script-mechanism side of the concept without dragging in broader force-close or HTLC timing behavior.

edges.rationale: The attack only makes sense once learners understand how Bitcoin retargets mining difficulty over long block windows.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the exploit tied to the retarget mechanism instead of burying it under generic mining vocabulary.

edges.rationale: Learners need the consensus timestamp constraints before they can reason about how miners manipulate time data and where the remaining attack surface lives.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the timestamp-boundary side of the exploit without overloading timelocks or block headers.

edges.rationale: Chaumian ecash is defined by using blind signatures to let a mint issue private bearer tokens without seeing the final token contents.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node tied to the specific cryptographic mechanism rather than treating it as generic digital-money history.

edges.rationale: Learners need the unsolved scarcity problem in digital cash before the trusted-mint tradeoff in Chaumian designs becomes meaningful.
edges.notes: Direct parent frames why the system still needed a central operator even though it improved privacy.

edges.rationale: The concept depends on understanding how money moved from bearer cash toward mediated account systems, so learners need the long-run monetary history first.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node tied to the bearer-versus-intermediated payment shift rather than drifting into generic civil-liberties framing.

edges.rationale: Learners need the general idea that payment systems can exclude or block participants before the loss of private cash as a payment right becomes fully meaningful.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the exclusion-risk side of the argument without reopening broader compliance or surveillance wrappers.

edges.rationale: The concept is about minimizing monetary distortion, so learners need to understand how inflation changes purchasing power and price signals first.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the measurement-distortion problem that ideal money is trying to avoid.

edges.rationale: The URI is a wrapper around a payment destination and amount, so learners need the underlying address and output model first.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node tied to the standard request format rather than collapsing it into higher-level wallet UX systems.

edges.rationale: Extended keys are one specific export and branch-derivation surface inside the broader BIP32 HD tree model, so learners need the base tree-derivation mechanics first.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node inside the HD derivation branch instead of treating xpub or xprv behavior as only a privacy or operational-wallet concept.

edges.rationale: Hardened derivation is a constraint on what an extended public key can derive and on how extended private and public key boundaries behave, so learners need the base xpub/xprv model first.
edges.notes: Direct parent closes the previously missing branch-level bridge between BIP32 generally and hardened derivation specifically.

edges.rationale: The escrow flow depends on understanding how multiple signers can authorize one spend and why a 2-of-3 threshold changes trust assumptions between buyer, seller, and arbitrator.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node tied to the threshold-signing contract pattern instead of treating it as generic dispute-resolution theory.

edges.rationale: The contract uses a P2SH redeem script to hide the multisig spending conditions until spend time, so learners need the P2SH wrapper first.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the on-chain contract packaging layer that makes the escrow pattern practical.

edges.rationale: WIF is a serialization format for one private key, so learners need the underlying asymmetric-key concept before the network prefix, checksum, and compression flag details make sense.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node anchored on key material itself instead of overfitting it to address formats or HD wallet hierarchies.

edges.rationale: Pool shares only make sense after learners understand why pooled mining exists and why miners need a lower-variance way to prove contributed work before an actual block is found.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node at the pooled-mining accounting layer without adding redundant proof-of-work or difficulty edges.

edges.rationale: The obscured commitment number is part of the same commitment-state machinery as the per-state secret chain, so learners need that state-derivation context before the field-level encoding scheme makes sense.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node in the narrow commitment-state branch instead of pulling in broader closure or HTLC behavior.

edges.rationale: Learners need the base HTLC idea before the offered and received witness-script branches, timeout paths, and preimage-claim paths become meaningful.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node tied to the payment-contract primitive instead of treating it as generic script trivia.

edges.rationale: HTLC output scripts are additional commitment-transaction outputs layered on top of the same to_local and to_remote commitment-script structure, so learners need that earlier script layer first.
edges.notes: Direct parent places the node at the next script layer above commitment outputs without dragging in later close-handling or watchtower flows.

edges.rationale: The bug is historically tied to duplicated coinbase transactions, so learners need the special structure and validation role of coinbase transactions before the replay vector makes sense.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the bug tied to the early-coinbase uniqueness failure instead of burying it under general mining history.

edges.rationale: Learners need the txid-plus-output-index reference model to understand why identical transaction ids could confuse later spends and overwrite assumptions in the UTXO set.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the reference-model side of the bug without conflating it with double-spend history or malleability.

edges.rationale: The bug only makes sense once learners understand how transaction hashes sit inside the block merkle tree and why internal-node preimages matter for block commitments.
edges.notes: One direct parent keeps the node at the block-commitment layer instead of reopening broader hashing or SPV overview branches.

edges.rationale: Learners need the general signing-and-verification mental model before the one-time Lamport construction and its tradeoffs become meaningful.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node tied to signature semantics instead of treating it as only a post-quantum curiosity.

edges.rationale: Lamport signatures replace elliptic-curve arithmetic with one-way hash preimages and comparisons, so learners need the hash primitive first.
edges.notes: Direct parent captures the actual cryptographic mechanism rather than forcing learners through unrelated elliptic-curve details.

edges.rationale: In Bitcoin practice the scheme is used as one backup-and-recovery method, so learners need the broader custody problem before the threshold-share design choice becomes meaningful.
edges.notes: Direct parent keeps the node anchored in practical custody use instead of drifting into a purely abstract cryptography topic.

edges.rationale: The transaction witness layout only makes sense after the learner understands that SegWit separates witness data from the legacy transaction serialization.
edges.notes: Direct edge keeps the node at the transaction-format layer; TXID/WTXID and witness-commitment consequences remain downstream concepts.

edges.rationale: Partial Merkle proofs reconstruct a transaction's inclusion path against the Merkle root carried by a block, so learners need block composition and root placement first.
edges.notes: The block-structure parent already inherits fundamentals.merkle-trees, avoiding a redundant direct edge.

edges.rationale: Review culture is applied throughout the proposal and implementation lifecycle, so learners need to understand the process in which Bitcoin changes are proposed and evaluated.
edges.notes: Direct edge supplies the development-lifecycle context without adding downstream release or deployment details.

edges.rationale: Independent review in Bitcoin is deliberately adversarial: reviewers look for correctness failures, attack paths, and compatibility hazards before changes are merged.
edges.notes: Direct edge keeps review distinct from responsible disclosure and reproducible-build verification, which are specialized downstream safeguards.

edges.rationale: Feature negotiation is used to advertise and interpret capabilities across Lightning peers and network announcements, so learners need the surrounding gossip and announcement context first.
edges.notes: Direct edge keeps the feature-bit mechanism distinct from the lower-level message transport and downstream routing protocols.

edges.rationale: Peers use a negotiated feature bit to advertise whether they can send and forward onion messages, so learners need the capability-negotiation model first.
edges.notes: Direct edge keeps the feature gate separate from the message forwarding mechanism.

edges.rationale: Onion messages use blinded paths to forward channel-independent payloads without exposing the final destination topology, so the route-blinding model must come first.
edges.notes: Payment onion routing and BOLT12 offers remain related sibling or downstream concepts rather than direct prerequisites.

edges.rationale: The Noise_XK handshake authenticates long-term Lightning node identities with public/private key material, so learners need that identity model first.
edges.notes: Direct edge keeps identity foundations separate from the session-key derivation mechanism.

edges.rationale: BOLT 08 uses elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman operations to derive shared session secrets, so the key-exchange primitive must be understood first.
edges.notes: Bitcoin P2P v2 transport remains a related sibling rather than a direct prerequisite because it is a different protocol context.

edges.rationale: Strict DER enforcement occurs while Script validates an ECDSA signature, so learners need the spending-condition and interpreter context first.
edges.notes: Direct edge keeps the rule at the script-validation layer rather than treating BIP 66 as a broad historical upgrade topic.

edges.rationale: The node builds on the wallet-level threshold signer policy before specifying a deterministic ordering convention for the signer keys.
edges.notes: Direct edge preserves the distinction between multisig policy and the BIP 67 interoperability rule.

edges.rationale: BIP 67 is commonly applied when constructing the redeem script whose hash is committed by P2SH, so the script-hash wrapper provides the output context.
edges.notes: The shared protocol.script ancestor remains inherited rather than a redundant direct edge.

edges.rationale: Rolling minimum fee escalation and transaction eviction are local consequences of the node's mempool relay policy, so learners need that policy boundary first.
edges.notes: The generic mempool, fee-market, and consensus-policy branches remain inherited or adjacent rather than direct prerequisites.

edges.rationale: BIP 322 proves message authorization by constructing virtual transactions that satisfy a Bitcoin output script, so learners need the Script spending-condition model first.
edges.notes: The digital-signature path is inherited through Script; PSBT, Schnorr, and Taproot remain related variants rather than redundant direct edges.

edges.rationale: RPC access security is the control boundary around Bitcoin Core's programmatic interface, so learners need to understand the interface and its authority before securing exposure to it.
edges.notes: Full-node operation is inherited through dev.bitcoin-core-rpc; wallet RPC and transaction-preflight nodes remain downstream use cases rather than direct parents.

edges.rationale: submitpackage is the admission counterpart to the existing testmempoolaccept preflight workflow, so learners need to understand local package checks before attempting submission and interpreting package results.
edges.notes: Package relay and cluster mempool remain neighboring concepts rather than direct prerequisites because this node focuses on one local RPC operation.

edges.rationale: Package RBF extends the ordinary replacement-policy model with package-level fee and cluster conditions, so learners need base RBF semantics first.
edges.notes: Package relay, cluster mempool, submitpackage, and pinning remain adjacent concepts rather than redundant direct edges.

edges.rationale: Cluster limits bound connected transaction sets, so learners need the cluster-mempool model before understanding why count and aggregate virtual-size limits replace legacy ancestor/descendant limits.
edges.notes: Package relay, Package RBF, CPFP, and version 3 relay remain affected workflows or neighboring policy mechanisms rather than direct prerequisites.

edges.rationale: Lightning DNS seeds specialize the general DNS bootstrap pattern with Lightning-specific query filters and known-peer lookup, so learners need the base DNS-seed model first.
edges.notes: Peer discovery is inherited through ops.dns-seeds; generic Bitcoin P2P bootstrapping remains a parent concept rather than a duplicated edge.

edges.rationale: BOLT 10 locates Lightning peers and filters results by Lightning address information, so learners need Lightning's node-discovery and gossip context before understanding the assisted-location mechanism.
edges.notes: Lightning routing and generic P2P messages are inherited through gossip rather than added as transitive prerequisites.

edges.rationale: The identifier names a Lightning channel's on-chain funding output, so learners need the channel and funding relationship first.
edges.notes: Direct channel context; the gossip protocol is a downstream consumer rather than a prerequisite.

edges.rationale: Short Channel IDs encode block height as their first component, so learners need the block-height concept before decoding the identifier.
edges.notes: The transaction-index and output-index components remain part of the identifier's own outcome rather than extra transitive edges.

edges.rationale: Miner decentralization only makes sense once learners distinguish miners' transaction-ordering role from full nodes' independent enforcement of consensus validity.
edges.notes: The edge keeps censorship power separate from authority to change consensus rules.

edges.rationale: Pool and solo mining explain the participant and coordination structure whose concentration or dispersion determines practical control over transaction ordering.
edges.notes: Stratum V2 remains optional implementation context, not a direct prerequisite.

edges.rationale: Selection cryptography evaluates cryptographic tools and dependencies through evidence, testing, authorship, and review quality, so learners need the broader Bitcoin review discipline first.
edges.notes: Adversarial thinking is inherited through dev.review-culture; reproducible builds are a downstream verification practice rather than a prerequisite.

edges.rationale: Block withholding exploits the difference between a pool-paid share and a share that also meets the network target, so learners must understand share accounting before the attack model makes sense.
edges.notes: Pool-versus-solo and mining economics are inherited through mining.pool-shares; do not add a redundant majority-attack edge.

edges.rationale: P2C tweaks a public key to commit to contract data, so learners need the public/private key model.
edges.notes: Taproot is adjacent, not a direct requirement.

edges.rationale: P2C derives a commitment tweak from contract data using a hash, so learners need hash commitments first.
edges.notes: The hash is part of the construction rather than generic background.

edges.rationale: Single-use seals rely on a UTXO's one-time spend property.
edges.notes: This explicit bridge prevents hiding the seal invariant inside generic UTXO semantics.

edges.rationale: Client-side validation relies on seals to prevent an offchain transition from being valid twice.
edges.notes: The UTXO parent is inherited through the seal and is intentionally not duplicated.

edges.rationale: Client-side validation uses cryptographic commitments to bind offchain asset rules and transitions, so P2C supplies the contract-anchoring mechanism.
edges.notes: Keep this direct edge: it prevents treating client-side validation as a vague UTXO-only extension.

edges.rationale: Replacement cycling repeatedly uses transaction replacement, so learners need the RBF policy mechanism first.
edges.notes: Package RBF is related but not required for the core attack sequence.

edges.rationale: The attack cycles child-fee relationships to evict a target, so learners need CPFP mechanics first.
edges.notes: Transaction pinning is the broader downstream risk class, not a prerequisite.

edges.rationale: Control blocks extend the tapleaf proof path with internal-key and parity data.
edges.notes: Generic Merkle trees are inherited through the proof node.

edges.rationale: Pseudonymous development relies on public technical evidence and independent peer review to preserve accountability without requiring legal-identity disclosure.
edges.notes: Privacy pseudonymity is related context, not a direct prerequisite, because this node concerns contributor identity and governance rather than transaction linkage.

edges.rationale: MAST uses a Merkle tree to commit to multiple alternative script branches while revealing only one branch during spending.
edges.notes: Direct cryptographic structure; TapTree remains a downstream Taproot-specific realization.

edges.rationale: MAST commits to alternative Bitcoin Script branches, so learners need the locking and spending language before the tree commitment is meaningful.
edges.notes: Keep Script direct; the node is not only a generic Merkle-tree concept.

edges.rationale: The quadratic hashing problem is a performance consequence of how legacy signature digests are constructed under SIGHASH semantics.
edges.notes: BIP 143 is a supporting mitigation source, not a prerequisite edge.

edges.rationale: The rogue-key attack occurs in naive public-key aggregation, so learners need the MuSig aggregation setting before the failure mode.
edges.notes: Schnorr is inherited through MuSig; avoid a redundant direct Schnorr edge.

edges.rationale: Selfish mining is an incentive strategy by miners and pools, so learners need the pooled-versus-solo mining context first.
edges.notes: Pool economics is one direct parent; chain selection is supplied by the finality parent below.

edges.rationale: Selfish mining relies on strategic withholding and release of competing branches, which requires the chain-reorganization and finality model.
edges.notes: Keep the reorganization model direct rather than adding generic proof-of-work as a redundant ancestor.

edges.rationale: Commit-delay-reveal composes a cryptographic commitment with a timed reveal, so the learner first needs the hiding and binding commitment model.
edges.notes: The delay/reveal composition is the node's distinct outcome.

edges.rationale: Dust attacks exploit the distinction between tiny-output relay policy and the ability to place trace-value UTXOs in a wallet.
edges.notes: Policy threshold is direct context for the attack mechanism.

edges.rationale: The privacy harm of dusting comes from linking future spends and wallet activity, so learners need the pseudonymity and linkage model.
edges.notes: Keep privacy linkage direct; it is not inherited through dust policy.

edges.rationale: Permissionless development depends on a neutral protocol that does not select which users or applications may participate.
edges.notes: Neutrality supplies the protocol property; the BIPs process supplies the change/proposal lifecycle below.

edges.rationale: Learners need the proposal and deployment process to distinguish building permissionlessly from changing Bitcoin's consensus rules without broad adoption.
edges.notes: Review culture remains related but is not a direct prerequisite for permissionless access.

edges.rationale: Bootstrappable builds extend reproducible builds by reducing trust in the compiler and build-tool binaries used to produce the reproducible output.
edges.notes: Keep this as a direct downstream edge; the node is not a duplicate alias for reproducible builds.

edges.rationale: Proof of Payment refers to a prior payment and reconstructs a transaction-shaped proof, so learners need the transaction lifecycle first.
edges.notes: Direct payment context; wallet UX and URI transport remain downstream or optional details.

edges.rationale: Pay to Anchor is a keyless refinement of the anchor-output and package-relay fee-bumping pattern, so learners need that broader context first.
edges.notes: BIP 431/TRUC is related anti-pinning context, not an inherent prerequisite.

decisions.subject: source.bitcoinops-topics
decisions.rationale: Use Bitcoin Optech Topics as a canonical naming registry, alias map, related-topic graph, and update feed rather than as a bulk node importer.
decisions.notes: Heavy existing reuse means most value is in dedupe and alias normalization.

decisions.subject: taproot-internal-bridge-concepts
decisions.rationale: Prioritize missing Taproot bridge nodes from the workshop instead of broad Taproot duplicates.
decisions.notes: This source is narrower but pedagogically stronger than the general topics index.

decisions.subject: implemented-first-node-slice
decisions.rationale: Start implementation with the tightest coherent subgraph: Taproot internal bridge concepts that were repeatedly surfaced across Optech and Decoding and had the lowest duplicate risk.
decisions.notes: Implemented in the current branch before the broader policy-bridge slice.

decisions.subject: source.btctranscripts-topics
decisions.rationale: Use BTC Transcripts for provenance, source-family mapping, and terminology normalization before minting new nodes.
decisions.notes: The topic index mixes concept nodes with source-format artifacts and organizations.

decisions.subject: source.bitcoindevs-decoding
decisions.rationale: Use Decoding as a sequencing and prerequisite-ordering source first, and only mint nodes from narrow lesson pages that survive dedupe.
decisions.notes: The strongest value is clean educational boundaries and beginner-to-intermediate ordering.

decisions.subject: source.bitcoin-dev-philosophy
decisions.rationale: Track the source by chapter containers rather than turning chapter titles into nodes. Split dense chapters later only when needed.
decisions.notes: Broad philosophy headers are too umbrella-like for direct ingestion.

decisions.subject: source.mastering-bitcoin-book-map
decisions.rationale: Track Mastering Bitcoin by chapter first and treat chapters as extraction containers, not node candidates.
decisions.notes: The book already has heavy overlap in current resources, so the main value is bridge concepts and prerequisite ladders.

decisions.subject: source-alias-normalization
decisions.rationale: Normalize aliases before adding nodes so jargon variants such as TRUC versus version 3 transaction relay and Offers versus BOLT12 Offers do not create duplicates.
decisions.notes: This is a core dedupe rule for the loop.

decisions.subject: protocol.consensus-vs-policy
decisions.rationale: The next bridge node after the Taproot-internal slice should be protocol.consensus-vs-policy because it gives the existing mempool and relay branch a missing conceptual parent without duplicating existing leaves.
decisions.notes: Implemented in the current branch. The immediate follow-up rewires target ops.mempool-policy, protocol.rbf, protocol.dust-policy, and protocol.cpfp-carve-out.

decisions.subject: privacy.address-reuse-harms
decisions.rationale: The existing privacy.address-reuse node already occupies the exact bridge position this candidate wants and already teaches the harm model.
decisions.notes: Future work should refine the existing node instead of minting a duplicate.

decisions.subject: protocol.timelock-taxonomy
decisions.rationale: protocol.timelocks already acts as the central bridge node for the absolute versus relative timelock family, so a second taxonomy wrapper would be redundant.
decisions.notes: Future work should enrich protocol.timelocks copy and resources instead of adding a second node.

decisions.subject: policy-subtree-parent-edges
decisions.rationale: The first policy-bridge rewire should target the nodes where the consensus-versus-policy boundary is most direct: ops.mempool-policy, protocol.rbf, protocol.dust-policy, and protocol.cpfp-carve-out. Deeper leaves should inherit the bridge transitively for now.
decisions.notes: This keeps the subtree clearer without over-wiring every downstream policy leaf at once.

decisions.subject: per-node-amazon-book-resource
decisions.rationale: Each node should expose at least one Amazon-linked book resource so learners have a long-form study option in addition to articles. When a node was already at its resource cap, the weakest non-book resource was replaced instead of bloating the list.
decisions.notes: The pass used only book links already established elsewhere in the repo, normalized chapter-link book entries to title-level Amazon links, and added a test to keep future nodes covered.

decisions.subject: protocol.version-3-transaction-relay
decisions.rationale: Version 3 transaction relay is the highest-confidence remaining concept from the audited seed sources because it sharpens the existing mempool policy branch without opening a new broad subtree. In the current graph it fits best as a direct child of transaction pinning, with the policy boundary already inherited transitively.
decisions.notes: Kept this slice additive for now instead of rewiring deeper descendants like protocol.ephemeral-anchors until the relay-policy branch grows further.

decisions.subject: validation-and-light-client-bridges
decisions.rationale: The next thin branch after the relay-policy slice is the trust and verification path. fundamentals.validation-sovereignty is the cleanest philosophy-side dependent to add now, and protocol.light-client-trust-model can be introduced as a narrow conceptual bridge without forcing rewires onto older phase-locked light-client nodes.
decisions.notes: Kept this slice additive after subagent review showed that rewiring legacy neighbors like protocol.bloom-filters-bip37 would add overlap without enough downstream surface yet.

decisions.subject: ops.full-node-vs-lightweight-wallet
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next consumer for protocol.light-client-trust-model is a user-facing comparison between full nodes and lightweight wallets. It strengthens the thin trust branch without forcing rewires on older phase-locked mechanism nodes.
decisions.notes: Chosen over further light-client neighbor rewires because phase-5, phase-6, and phase-11 constraints make those edits more brittle than adding a new downstream consumer.

decisions.subject: protocol.chain-split-risk
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next philosophy-derived additive slice is protocol.chain-split-risk. It isolates one specific upgrade-risk outcome from the broader deployment-mechanisms discussion and creates a bridge from protocol.softfork-hardfork to the existing activation-history case studies without duplicating them.
decisions.notes: Chosen over a broader future-deployment-mechanisms wrapper because the split-cost concept is more atomic and easier to place cleanly in the current graph.

decisions.subject: protocol.tapscript
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Taproot-derived additive slice is protocol.tapscript. It captures the specific BIP342 script ruleset without duplicating generic Taproot overview material or forcing rewires onto older phase-locked descendants such as protocol.op-checksigadd.
decisions.notes: Chosen over a synthetic taproot-script-path-spends wrapper because Tapscript is a canonical source-native concept with cleaner boundaries in the current graph. The direct protocol.script edge was dropped after the redundancy check showed it was already inherited transitively through the Taproot branch.

decisions.subject: dev.taproot-psbt-fields
decisions.rationale: The cleanest additive PSBT/Taproot slice is dev.taproot-psbt-fields. It captures the BIP371 extension surface directly without drifting into general PSBT mechanics, descriptor transport, or MuSig-specific coordination flows.
decisions.notes: Implemented in the current branch after protocol.tapscript as the next narrow Taproot-adjacent wallet-development slice. The direct dev.psbt-v2 edge was dropped because BIP371 applies to PSBTv0 and PSBTv2.

decisions.subject: protocol.accidental-confiscation
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next upgrade-risk slice from the audited source set is protocol.accidental-confiscation. It captures one specific soft-fork failure mode without expanding back into the broader activation-mechanism or governance-history wrappers already represented elsewhere in the graph.
decisions.notes: Chosen over a broader soft-fork-activation wrapper because accidental confiscation is narrower, source-native, and easier to place without duplicating versionbits and activation-history leaves.

decisions.subject: custody.coin-selection
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next wallet-side bridge from the audited source set is custody.coin-selection. It isolates the wallet's UTXO-choice layer without collapsing into manual coin-control UX, privacy heuristics, or implementation-specific RPC behavior.
decisions.notes: Chosen over philosophy-side wrappers such as full-node decentralization because coin selection has clearer boundaries and stronger immediate downstream leverage in the current graph. The direct protocol.utxo-model edge was dropped after the redundancy check showed it was already inherited through the fee-market branch.

decisions.subject: dev.taproot-descriptors
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Optech-side additive slice is dev.taproot-descriptors. It captures the BIP386 tr() descriptor layer directly without collapsing into generic descriptor wallet flows, PSBT transport, or MuSig policy composition.
decisions.notes: Chosen over broader governance wrappers and over deeper Taproot internals because it adds a clean new bridge between existing descriptor and Taproot branches without forcing rewires on older wallet nodes.

decisions.subject: protocol.taproot-key-path-spends
decisions.rationale: The cleanest remaining workshop-native Taproot slice is protocol.taproot-key-path-spends. It captures one specific spending mode already implied by the existing Taproot and TapTweak nodes without collapsing back into the broad Taproot overview or forward into script-path-only nodes such as Tapscript and TapTree.
decisions.notes: Kept the direct prerequisite set to TapTweak only after the redundancy check showed Schnorr and Taproot context are already inherited transitively, and script-path branches are sibling alternatives rather than prerequisites.

decisions.subject: protocol.p2pk
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Decoding-derived additive slice is protocol.p2pk. It fills a specific historical script-template gap without forcing direct-edge violations or overlapping too heavily with existing wallet-level multisig coverage.
decisions.notes: Deferred protocol.p2ms because its cleanest current prerequisite options are either wallet-semantic overlap or a curricular, non-direct dependency on protocol.p2pk.

decisions.subject: security.nonce-reuse-attack
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Decoding-derived additive slice after P2PK is security.nonce-reuse-attack. It adds a precise Schnorr signing failure mode without reopening the brittle Taproot prerequisite chain or forcing a phase-11 custody rewire.
decisions.notes: Kept additive and separate from security.exfiltration-resistant-signing so the graph distinguishes the failure mode from the defense pattern.

decisions.subject: protocol.p2pkh
decisions.rationale: The next clean Decoding-derived output-template slice is protocol.p2pkh. It fills an important historical and conceptual gap between direct public-key locking and later script-hash patterns without collapsing into wallet UX or broad address-format overviews.
decisions.notes: This does not resolve the protocol-side bridge for protocol.p2ms by itself. The likely next comparison remains protocol.op-checkmultisig versus protocol.p2ms directly.

decisions.subject: protocol.fee-calculation
decisions.rationale: The cleanest remaining Decoding-derived transactions slice is protocol.fee-calculation. It isolates how fees are derived and interpreted without collapsing into the broader transaction-construction workflow, the economics of fee markets, or wallet-specific estimation tooling.
decisions.notes: Chosen before more bundled transaction pages because it survives dedupe as one precise learner-facing mechanism and strengthens the path into fee-market and fee-bumping nodes. The direct parent set was later reduced to protocol.block-weight-vbytes only after the redundancy check showed UTXO accounting was already inherited transitively.

decisions.subject: protocol.bech32m-addresses
decisions.rationale: The cleanest remaining Mastering Bitcoin address-format slice is protocol.bech32m-addresses. It captures the checksum and witness-version distinction for modern native segwit addresses without collapsing into the broad addresses-and-outputs umbrella or into Taproot internals that belong elsewhere.
decisions.notes: Chosen over broader philosophy-side wrappers because it is a precise learner-facing gap with low duplicate risk and a clear place in the current graph.

decisions.subject: protocol.script-execution-stack
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Mastering Bitcoin bridge concept is protocol.script-execution-stack. It gives the current Script subtree an explicit mechanism layer between the broad Script overview and opcode-specific leaves, which improves gap diagnosis without inflating the graph with another umbrella node.
decisions.notes: Chosen after the Bech32m address slice because it survives dedupe, has one clean direct prerequisite, and strengthens an already active concept branch.

decisions.subject: protocol.scriptless-scripts
decisions.rationale: Do not add scriptless scripts as a standalone node. In the current graph it remains a cross-domain umbrella label spanning adaptor signatures, DLEQ-assisted constructions, atomic swaps, PTLCs, DLCs, and related privacy-preserving contract techniques rather than one atomic learner-facing concept boundary.
decisions.notes: Merged into protocol.adaptor-signatures for search discoverability after confirming the term is broader than Lightning and broader than any one downstream application.

decisions.subject: lightning.per-commitment-secrets
decisions.rationale: The cleanest first additive slice from Programming Lightning is lightning.per-commitment-secrets. It captures one specific state-derivation mechanism that the current graph depends on implicitly through commitment and revocation nodes, without reopening the broader Lightning Tutorial wrappers around channels, HTLCs, or routing.
decisions.notes: Chosen over broader Programming Lightning candidates such as payment channels, onion-routing internals, or HTLC workflow wrappers because it survives dedupe as one precise mechanism with clear downstream leverage. The direct parent set was later reduced to lightning.commitment-transactions only after the redundancy check showed the crypto primitives were already inherited transitively.

decisions.subject: protocol.time-warp-attack
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Bitcoin Dev Project Explained slice is protocol.time-warp-attack. It captures a specific consensus exploit at the boundary between difficulty retargeting and timestamp rules without collapsing into the broader difficulty-adjustment or median-time-past overview nodes.
decisions.notes: Chosen over broader or already-covered explained posts because the exploit is atomic, learner-diagnosable, and still missing from the current graph even though nearby consensus parents already exist.

decisions.subject: history.chaumian-ecash
decisions.rationale: Add history.chaumian-ecash. Current coverage only mentions DigiCash inside history.pre-bitcoin-digital-cash and covers modern Bitcoin-backed mints in extension.ecash; the dedicated historical bridge is still missing.
decisions.notes: Chosen over cypherpunk manifesto or monetary-theory slices because this one survives dedupe as a precise learner gap between blind-signature cryptography, pre-Bitcoin digital-cash history, and modern ecash descendants.

decisions.subject: history.cypherpunk-manifesto
decisions.rationale: Merge A Cypherpunk's Manifesto into history.cypherpunks; do not add a separate manifesto node.
decisions.notes: The manifesto is a foundational primary source for the movement-level history node, but it does not survive dedupe as a separate atomic concept once the graph already has the Cypherpunk Movement bridge node.

decisions.subject: history.crypto-anarchist-manifesto
decisions.rationale: Merge The Crypto Anarchist Manifesto into history.bitcoin-anarchism; do not add a separate manifesto node.
decisions.notes: The manifesto is a foundational primary source for Bitcoin's anarchist and cypherpunk framing, but it works better as discoverability and sourcing on the existing node than as a duplicate manifesto-level concept.

decisions.subject: privacy.secret-right-to-cash
decisions.rationale: Add privacy.secret-right-to-cash. It isolates the bearer-cash privacy and exclusion tradeoff directly, with clear parents in monetary history and censorship resistance, while the remaining Cypherpunk Library money-theory texts overlap more heavily with existing economics nodes.
decisions.notes: Chosen over asymptotically-ideal-money and other adjacent library texts because the graph already has denser monetary-theory coverage than bearer-cash privacy coverage.

decisions.subject: economics.ideal-money
decisions.rationale: Add economics.ideal-money. It isolates money's role as a stable measuring stick for economic calculation, which survives dedupe more cleanly than the remaining merge-bound privacy material and the still-parentless DKG path.
decisions.notes: Kept neutrality and Hayek-side political-economy framing as neighboring context rather than direct prerequisites so this lands as a distinct measurement-and-calculation concept instead of collapsing into the existing monetary-theory lane.

decisions.subject: audit.current-source-pass
decisions.rationale: The current audited pass across the tracked Programming Lightning, Decoding, Optech workshop, Cypherpunk Library starting clusters, Bitcoin Dev Project explained posts, Bitcoin Design clusters, and Bitcoin Core Academy clusters is exhausted for clean additive nodes. The remaining visible items are merge-only or still defer-bound, so the next move is source expansion rather than forcing a weak node into the graph.
decisions.notes: Recorded after three subagent comparisons converged on July 9, 2026: no remaining candidate from these audited families survives dedupe more cleanly than the already-landed nodes, so the audit should widen to the Bitcoin Developer Guides next.

decisions.subject: protocol.bitcoin-uri-bip21
decisions.rationale: The first Bitcoin Developer Guides slice should be protocol.bitcoin-uri-bip21. It captures a specific missing payment-request standard with one clean direct parent, while the nearby escrow/arbitration and pool-share candidates remain valid but sit deeper inside broader contract and mining mechanism branches.
decisions.notes: Chosen after the first Developer Guides subagent pass surfaced three viable candidates. Kept QR-code transport and BIP70-specific behavior out of scope so the node stays focused on the BIP21 request format itself.

decisions.subject: dev.hardened-keys
decisions.rationale: The next Bitcoin Developer Guides slice should be dev.hardened-keys. It captures a specific missing BIP32 sub-concept with one clean direct parent, while the remaining escrow/arbitration and pool-share candidates remain valid but live deeper inside broader contract and mining mechanism branches.
decisions.notes: Chosen after the Wallets subagent identified Hardened Keys as the cleanest additive bridge inside the existing HD derivation branch. After later book-side extraction exposed dev.extended-keys as a missing bridge, the node was re-anchored there so BIP44 path structure and xpub-leakage consequences could stay out of scope while the hardened derivation mechanism remained direct.

decisions.subject: dev.extended-keys
decisions.rationale: The cleanest next Mastering Bitcoin wallet-branch slice is dev.extended-keys. It captures the xpub/xprv boundary directly without collapsing into generic BIP32 coverage, single-key serialization formats like WIF, or downstream privacy and watch-only consequences.
decisions.notes: Chosen after compare-pass subagents ruled out a standalone Base58Check node and confirmed that the missing branch-level xpub/xprv concept would strengthen the existing BIP32, hardened-keys, and wallet-policy subtree more cleanly.

decisions.subject: protocol.escrow-and-arbitration
decisions.rationale: The next Bitcoin Developer Guides slice should be protocol.escrow-and-arbitration. It captures a missing contract-pattern node that bridges the existing multisig and P2SH primitives into one concrete adjudicated-spend flow, while pool-shares remains a valid but more isolated mining leaf.
decisions.notes: Chosen after the Contracts subagent revalidated the direct parents against the current branch and after the wallet candidate Hardened Keys had already been landed. Kept Lightning payment channels and CoinJoin out of scope so the node stays focused on the 2-of-3 escrow pattern itself.

decisions.subject: dev.wallet-import-format-wif
decisions.rationale: The next Bitcoin Developer Guides slice should be dev.wallet-import-format-wif. It captures a missing named wallet serialization standard with one clean direct parent and lower dedupe risk than the remaining pool-shares leaf.
decisions.notes: Chosen after a follow-up Wallet Files pass in the Developer Guides and after subagent comparison against mining.pool-shares. Kept address formats, P2PKH, and HD wallet hierarchy concepts out of the direct prerequisite set so the node stays focused on single-key import/export serialization.

decisions.subject: mining.pool-shares
decisions.rationale: The next Bitcoin Developer Guides slice should be mining.pool-shares. It captures the pooled-mining accounting proof directly with one clean direct parent, and it survives dedupe more cleanly than folding it into either the pool-vs-solo comparison or the later Stratum and getblocktemplate transport nodes.
decisions.notes: Chosen after the Wallet Files follow-up had already landed WIF and after subagent comparison revalidated that pool shares are the remaining additive Developer Guides mining leaf. Kept proof-of-work and difficulty edges inherited through mining.pool-vs-solo so the new node stays free of redundant direct prerequisites.

decisions.subject: lightning.obscured-commitment-number
decisions.rationale: Add. Distinct BOLT 3 state-number encoding mechanism for commitment transactions; lower dedupe risk than revocation, close, or HTLC wrapper lessons already covered.
decisions.notes: Chosen after per-commitment-secrets because the commitment-transactions module exposes dedicated obscure-factor and obscured-commitment-number exercises, making this a cleaner atomic follow-up than broader wrapper lessons.

decisions.subject: lightning.commitment-output-scripts
decisions.rationale: Add. Distinct BOLT 3 commitment-output script layer for to_local/to_remote and CSV delay; lower dedupe risk than revocation, close, or HTLC wrapper lessons already covered.
decisions.notes: Chosen after the first two Programming Lightning commitment-state slices because the source exposes dedicated static-remotekey and CSV checkpoints, making the output-script layer a cleaner atomic addition than broader revocation, close, or HTLC wrappers.

decisions.subject: lightning.htlc-output-scripts
decisions.rationale: Add lightning.htlc-output-scripts. It isolates the offered/received HTLC witness-script layer directly, bridging the current gap between generic HTLC concepts and HTLC timeout settlement without collapsing into broader routing or channel-policy wrappers.
decisions.notes: Chosen over economics.ideal-money because the Lightning branch already has the parent and child surfaces needed for this script-layer concept to land as a clean, reusable node today.

decisions.subject: protocol.duplicate-transaction-bug
decisions.rationale: Add. Same explained-post consensus-cleanup lane as protocol.time-warp-attack, distinct from existing double-spend and malleability nodes, and cleaner than DKG/Shamir/Lamport/garbled-circuits given current graph parents.
decisions.notes: Chosen as the next X explained-post slice because the duplicate-transaction bug survives dedupe as a specific consensus-cleanup failure mode with clearer direct parents than the currently visible custody or advanced-crypto candidates.

decisions.subject: protocol.64-byte-transaction-bug
decisions.rationale: Add. Same explained-post consensus-cleanup lane; distinct Merkle-commitment failure with one clean direct parent and lower dedupe risk than DKG/Shamir/Lamport/garbled-circuit candidates.
decisions.notes: Chosen as the next X explained-post slice because it preserves the consensus-cleanup bug branch while staying atomic, with a cleaner parent than a broader Merkle-vulnerabilities wrapper or the more duplicate-prone custody and advanced-crypto candidates.

decisions.subject: protocol.off-by-one-difficulty-bug
decisions.rationale: Merge Off-by-one difficulty bug into protocol.time-warp-attack; do not add a separate node.
